executive_summary.one_paragraph: SRC is canonically a cytoplasmic-face, inner-leaflet-anchored kinase with no extracellular domain. Two 2025 studies (PMID:41818370, PMID:41818382) report a cancer-specific non-canonical topological inversion (eSrc) via autophagolysosomal exocytosis, placing SRC on the outer cell surface in tumor cells and primary tumors, with anti-Src antibody killing confirmed in xenograft models. This surface form is strictly disease-state-induced; normal cells retain canonical inner-leaflet topology and are inaccessible. Evidence grade is conflicting: strong preclinical functional data for eSrc versus well-established canonical intracellular topology. Surface accessibility is therefore moderate and highly state-dependent (cancer-selective).

surface_evidence.grade_rationale: Two independent sources (PMID:41818370, PMID:41818382) assert that SRC undergoes non-canonical topological inversion to appear on the outer cancer-cell surface (eSrc), supported by functional antibody-killing assays in xenograft models. A surfaceome MS study (PMC12777226) also captured SRC in a surface-glycoprotein fraction. However, strong canonical topology evidence (PMC11399299) establishes that SRC-family kinases are tethered via acyl groups to the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane with no extracellular domain — directly conflicting with extracellular accessibility as the default state. The surface evidence is all from a single recent study cluster (review assertions + functional assay from same lab/abstract), while inner-leaflet canonical topology is well-established biochemically. The sEV detection (PMC10356899) is ambiguous (inner-leaflet vesicle association). This combination of a meaningful extracellular-inversion claim versus strong canonical intracellular-face topology fits the 'conflicting' grade.

expression_observations.context: Embryonic chicken limb bud-derived chondrogenic cells; sialoglycoprotein enrichment (aminooxy-biotin conjugation) + LC-MS/MS; SRC detected in surfaceome cluster 2 across differentiation time points
High invadopodia activity cancer cell lines; SRC detected in small extracellular vesicle (sEV) proteomics cluster 2 alongside CTTN, CFL1, ITGA3, ITGB3
HaCaT keratinocytes; whole-cell lysate western blot for total SRC and phospho-SRC (Y416); no subcellular fractionation

MC3T3-E1 osteoblast-like cells under fluid shear stress; c-Src Tyr416 phosphorylation detected; RANKL redistribution to membrane fraction by subcellular fractionation + WB; SRC itself not directly fractionated

Cancer cell lines (unspecified) and primary tumor tissues / mouse xenograft models; noncanonical translocation/inversion of SRC onto outer cell surface demonstrated in vitro and in vivo; anti-Src antibody-based therapy mediates tumor cell killing
Primary tumor tissues; extracellular membrane-associated Src (eSrc) detected; anti-Src antibody therapeutic engagement of extracellular face confirmed
Mouse xenograft models; anti-Src antibody-based therapy mediated tumor cell killing confirming surface SRC accessibility in vivo
Cancer cells; exocytosis-mediated exposure of Src at outer cell surface; therapeutic targeting context
Unspecified cell type; SRC named alongside ERBB2, CSF1R in a surfaceome functional cluster in a surface-proteomics dataset; review-level secondary assertion

non_surface_expression.context: HaCaT keratinocytes — whole-cell lysate western blot for total SRC and phospho-SRC (Y416)
MC3T3-E1 osteoblast-like cells — subcellular fractionation + WB showing c-Src activation (Tyr416) and membrane-fraction redistribution under fluid shear stress

therapeutic_engagement.description: Anti-Src antibody-based therapies targeting extracellular membrane-associated Src (eSrc) have demonstrated tumor cell killing in cell culture systems and mouse xenograft models (PMID:41818370). A parallel study describes exocytosis-exposed Src on cancer cell surfaces as a therapeutic target (PMID:41818382). No named clinical-stage antibody or ADC is documented in the ledger; the programs are described at the preclinical in vivo stage.
therapeutic_engagement.surface_form_rationale: Canonically, SRC is tethered via N-myristoylation/palmitoylation to the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane with no extracellular domain. In cancer cells, a non-canonical topological inversion (eSrc) exposes the protein on the outer cell surface via an exocytosis/inversion mechanism. The anti-Src antibody therapeutics described in the ledger engage this cancer-specific surface-exposed (inverted) form, not a secreted isoform.

contradicting_evidence.claim: SRC is canonically tethered via N-myristoylation and palmitoylation to the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane, making it a cytoplasmic-face kinase with no extracellular domain, no transmembrane helices, and cytoplasmic N- and C-termini. This topology means SRC is not surface-accessible under normal conditions, directly contradicting a surface-accessibility hypothesis. The cancer-specific extracellular inversion (eSrc) represents a non-canonical exception rather than the baseline topology.
contradicting_evidence.likely_explanation: The canonical inner-leaflet anchorage places SRC entirely on the cytoplasmic face, so conventional antibody-based surface targeting would fail. The eSrc phenomenon in cancer cells may represent a minority population or context-specific reorientation; non-permeabilized staining in relevant cancer cell lines would be needed to confirm whether any extracellular epitope is genuinely accessible.

cell_states.descriptor: In cancer cells (in vitro and primary tumors), SRC is noncanonically translocated via autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE) to the outer cell surface, creating an extracellular membrane-associated pool (eSrc) absent in normal cells.

Upon TCR-pMHC engagement, SRC-family kinases are enriched in zones of close membrane contact where inhibitory phosphatases (CD45, CD148) are spatially excluded, increasing effective inner-leaflet SRC activity relative to resting T cells.
Fluid shear stress in osteoblast-like MC3T3-E1 cells activates c-Src (Tyr416 phosphorylation), driving redistribution of RANKL from intracellular stores toward membrane-proximal regions.

Glioblastoma cells with high invadopodia activity show enrichment of SRC in secreted small extracellular vesicles, indicating disease-state-associated redistribution of SRC toward the extracellular vesicle fraction.

dual_localization.condition: in cancer cells (disease state); delivered via autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE)
in glioblastoma cells with high invadopodia activity

membrane_subdomains.subdomain: inner leaflet of plasma membrane (acyl-anchored, cytoplasmic face)

anatomical_accessibility.context: cancer cells (cell lines and primary tumors) — eSrc outer-surface exposure
anatomical_accessibility.rationale: In cancer, SRC is noncanonically translocated via autophagolysosomal exocytosis to the outer plasma membrane leaflet (eSrc), making it extracellularly accessible. Systemically delivered antibodies can reach this surface-exposed epitope, validated by anti-Src ADC killing in xenograft models.

anatomical_accessibility.context: normal (non-cancer) cells — canonical inner-leaflet membrane association
anatomical_accessibility.rationale: In normal cells SRC is tethered via N-myristoylation to the cytoplasmic/inner leaflet of the plasma membrane. It has no extracellular domain; systemic binders cannot access it from the bloodstream in the absence of the cancer-specific surface-inversion event.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Non-cancer cells: SRC is canonically tethered via N-myristoylation to the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane, inaccessible from the extracellular side.
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Cancer cells (in vitro cell lines and primary tumors in vivo)
accessibility_modulation.change: SRC undergoes noncanonical translocation and inversion onto the outer cell surface (eSrc), acquiring an extracellular membrane-associated topology that is absent in normal cells. This is mechanistically driven by autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE).
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: eSrc is a cancer-specific extracellular antigen fully accessible to antibodies and other extracellular binders. Anti-Src antibody therapies mediated tumor cell killing in culture and xenograft models, confirming therapeutic accessibility from outside the cell.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Non-cancer cells: SRC resides at the cytoplasmic face of the plasma membrane; no autophagolysosomal exocytosis of SRC documented.
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Cancer cell lines with active autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE)
accessibility_modulation.change: ALE delivers SRC from intracellular/inner-leaflet reservoir to the outer cell surface, creating an extracellular-facing pool (eSrc) specifically in cancer cells.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: ALE-dependent surface exposure makes SRC accessible to extracellular binders only in cancer cells where this exocytotic pathway is active, providing a cancer-selective therapeutic window.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Cancer cells at rest: SRC is present at the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane and within intracellular compartments including autophagolysosomal stores.
accessibility_modulation.change: SRC redistributes from intracellular autophagolysosomal stores to the outer plasma membrane surface (eSrc), creating a dual pool: cytoplasmic-face membrane-associated SRC and extracellular-facing eSrc.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Only the eSrc outer-surface pool is accessible to extracellular binders; the inner-leaflet pool remains inaccessible without permeabilization. Binder selectivity may depend on cancer-state ALE activity.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Resting T cells: constitutive phosphatase activity (CD45, CD148) suppresses TCR signaling; SRC-family kinases are at the inner plasma membrane leaflet but functionally inhibited.
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: TCR-activated T cells upon pMHC engagement
accessibility_modulation.change: TCR-pMHC interaction traps the receptor in small close-contact membrane zones that exclude large phosphatases (CD45, CD148) but retain SRC-family kinases, enabling SRC-dependent tyrosine phosphorylation of TCR ITAMs.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: SRC itself remains on the inner leaflet and is not extracellularly accessible; however, its functional availability at specific membrane microdomains is state-dependent, relevant for intracellular targeting strategies.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Resting osteoblast-like MC3T3-E1 cells: c-Src at baseline activation state; RANKL predominantly in intracellular stores.
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Fluid shear stress-stimulated MC3T3-E1 osteoblast-like cells
accessibility_modulation.change: Fluid shear stress activates c-Src (Tyr416 phosphorylation), which drives redistribution of RANKL from intracellular lysosome-like stores toward cell periphery and membrane fraction. SRC kinase activity is the upstream modulating event.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: SRC itself does not acquire novel surface exposure; rather, activated SRC at the inner membrane leaflet orchestrates surface translocation of downstream targets (RANKL). SRC remains inaccessible extracellularly in this context.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Normal glioblastoma cells or low-invadopodia-activity GBM lines: SRC at inner-leaflet plasma membrane with minimal secretion into extracellular vesicles.
accessibility_modulation.change: In invasive GBM cells with active invadopodia, SRC is enriched in small extracellular vesicles (sEVs) secreted by these cells, representing redistribution of SRC protein toward extracellular vesicle compartments.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: SRC associated with secreted sEVs may be accessible on the outer vesicle surface in GBM disease contexts with high invadopodia activity, potentially offering a disease-state-specific extracellular target on vesicles rather than the cell surface itself.

structure.source: AlphaFold DB (whole-protein pLDDT — no ECD residues in topology)

surface_bind.attribution: © Marchand, Khakzad, Correia et al. — EPFL / Inria / Novo Nordisk

co_receptor_requirements.rationale: ALE-mediated surface translocation of eSrc may require intact autophagolysosomal pathway components active in cancer cells, but no specific obligate co-receptor for surface expression has been identified in the ledger. Dependency is mechanistically plausible but uncharacterized.

shed_form.mechanism: SRC is enriched in small extracellular vesicles (sEVs) from high-invadopodia glioblastoma lines; inner-leaflet vesicle association rather than classical proteolytic shedding

restricted_subdomain.rationale: eSrc surface exposure is exclusively cancer-cell-specific, driven by ALE in transformed cells. Normal (non-cancer) cells retain canonical inner-leaflet topology with no extracellular accessibility. This is a disease-state restriction, not a classic membrane subdomain restriction, but functionally limits systemic binder access to cancer cells only.

ecd_size_assessment.rationale: SRC has no canonical extracellular domain — it is a cytoplasmic-face kinase with no TM helices and no ECD in normal topology. In cancer-specific eSrc inversion, the kinase domain is extracellularly exposed but the accessible surface is defined by the inverted cytoplasmic kinase fold, not a canonical ECD.

epitope_masking.rationale: The eSrc extracellular-facing kinase domain was not evolved as an extracellular antigen; conformational constraints of the inverted kinase fold may limit accessible epitopes for antibody binding. No direct epitope-masking data in the ledger; inferred from non-canonical topology.

evidence.claim: Src is reported to be noncanonically translocated and inverted onto the outer cell surface in cancer cells, both in cell culture systems and in vivo tumor models. This represents a direct surface-expression claim for a canonically intracellular kinase, constituting the primary surface-accessibility finding for SRC in this study.

spans.quote: In this work, we found that Src is noncanonically translocated and inverted onto the cell surface in cancer, both in vitro and in vivo.

evidence.claim: Extracellular membrane-associated Src (eSrc) was detected in primary tumor tissues. Anti-Src antibody-based therapies achieved tumor cell killing in cell culture systems and mouse xenograft models, directly demonstrating therapeutic engagement of the extracellular face of SRC on cancer cells. This constitutes a preclinical antibody therapeutic engagement claim for cell-surface SRC.

spans.quote: Furthermore, this extracellular membrane-associated Src (eSrc) was found in primary tumors, and anti-Src antibody-based therapies mediated tumor cell killing in cell culture systems and in mouse xenograft models.

evidence.claim: N-myristoylated Src undergoes topological inversion to appear on the outer cell surface in cancer. The N-myristoyl lipid anchor, which normally tethers Src to the cytoplasmic leaflet of the plasma membrane, participates in this inversion mechanism. This defines a non-canonical topology for surface SRC distinct from classical type I/II transmembrane topology, with the kinase domain potentially accessible to extracellular antibodies.

spans.quote: Thus, intracellular N-myristoylated proteins, prototypically Src, can be topologically inverted onto the cell surface in cancer and targeted with antibody therapeutics.

evidence.claim: A parallel study reports that exocytosis exposes Src at the outer surface of cancer cells, poised for therapeutic targeting. This is an independent abstract-level surface-expression claim for cancer-cell surface SRC, supporting the inverted-Src model from a second paper and constituting a second independent source for the eSrc surface-accessibility finding.

spans.quote: Exocytosis exposes Src at the outer surface of cancer cells, poised for therapeutic targeting.

evidence.claim: SRC-family kinases (including SRC) are tethered via acyl groups (N-myristoylation and palmitoylation) to the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane. This establishes that canonically, SRC is a cytoplasmic-face membrane-associated kinase with no extracellular domain, zero TM helices, and cytoplasmic N- and C-termini — corroborating DeepTMHMM's topology call. The inner-leaflet tethering is the baseline topology against which the cancer-specific extracellular inversion (eSrc) should be interpreted.

spans.quote: These motifs are phosphorylated by SRC-family tyrosine kinases, which are tethered via acyl groups to the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane.

evidence.claim: SRC kinase was identified in surfaceome cluster 2 in a mass-spectrometry-based surfaceome study of embryonic chicken limb bud-derived chondrogenic cells, using sialoglycoprotein enrichment (aminooxy-biotin conjugation) plus LC-MS/MS. SRC detection in the surfaceome fraction suggests at least a sub-population of SRC is captured by this surface-glycoprotein enrichment approach in chondrogenic cells, although SRC lacks canonical N-glycosylation sites, so the detection may reflect co-enrichment with glycoprotein complexes or a minor surface-inverted fraction.

spans.quote: Surfaceome cluster 2 harbors several cell-surface regulators of chondrocyte biology, including RECK (a membrane-anchored matrix metalloprotease inhibitor [ 45 ]), FGFR2 (fibroblast growth factor receptor 2; regulates chondrocyte proliferation/differentiation signaling [ 46 ]), multiple ephrin receptors (EPHA7, EPHB2, EPHB1, EPHB3 with potential roles in cartilage morphogenesis [ 47 ]), SRC kinase (chondrocyte phenotype control [ 48 ]), and GPC1 (glypical-1

evidence.claim: Surfaceome profiling used sialoglycoprotein enrichment via aminooxy-biotin conjugation followed by high-resolution LC-MS/MS on 3 biological replicates at multiple differentiation time points. This is the methodological pairing for the SRC surfaceome-cluster result (draft_PMC12777226_results_01): cell surface capture chemistry targeting N-glycosylated proteins, no cell permeabilization.

spans.quote: Temporal proteomic profiling was conducted at days 1, 3, 6, 10, and 15 of micromass culture corresponding to key histodifferentiation stages [ 17 , 18 ], combining sialoglycoprotein enrichment via aminooxy-biotin conjugation with high-resolution LC-MS/MS on 3 biological replicates ( n = 3).

evidence.claim: In a surfaceome-context discussion, SRC is named alongside classical surface receptor kinases (ERBB2, CSF1R) as a non-receptor signaling kinase identified within the same functional cluster of a surface-proteomics dataset. This is a secondary review-level assertion that SRC appeared in a surfaceome dataset, supporting but not directly evidencing cell-surface localization.

spans.quote: The ephrin/plexin group was part of a larger functional cluster with roles in chemokine signaling and increased chemotaxis 34 , 35 that included not just well-known receptor/non-receptor signaling kinases (e.g., ERBB2, CSF1R, SRC, PRKCD), but also proteins with functionally diverse activities (i.e., proteins with roles in immune response, cell adhesion cell-ECM binding molecules, and transport).

evidence.claim: SRC was detected with increased abundance in small extracellular vesicles (sEVs) secreted by high-invadopodia-activity cancer cell lines, in a proteomics cluster that also contains integrins and cytoskeletal regulators. This represents a shed/secreted form of SRC packaged into sEVs, indicating that a fraction of total cellular SRC is released in membrane-enclosed particles. This is an ambiguous surface/shed-form observation: sEV-associated SRC may reflect inner-leaflet membrane association within vesicles rather than extracellular-face exposure.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: high invadopodia activity cancer cell lines

spans.quote: Furthermore, these high invadopodia activity cell lines secreted sEVs with a greater abundance of proteins involved in the regulation of invadopodia formation (CTTN, CFL1, SRC, ITGA3, ITGB3 – Cluster 2) and proteolytic activity (MMP2, MMP14, BSG/CD147 – Cluster 2) (Fig. 3 D).

evidence.claim: Western blot detection of total SRC protein and phospho-SRC (Y416) in HaCaT keratinocytes (no subcellular fractionation described). This is a whole-cell lysate WB observation with no paired surface fractionation step; it provides non-surface protein expression evidence only and feeds the non_surface_expression list. Antibody identifiers not specified in this clip.

spans.quote: n = 2, representative pictures. b qRT-PCR analyses of CYP1A1 and AKR1C3 in HaCaT-shAHR and HaCaT-EV cells treated with 10 μM PP2 or 0.1 % DMSO for 24 h. n = 4. *, p ≤ 0.05 compared to EV DMSO, #, p ≤ 0.05 compared to EV B[a]P. c qRT-PCR analyses of CYP1A1 and AKR1C3 in HaCaT-shAHR and HaCaT-EV keratinocytes treated as indicated for 24 h. n = 4–7. *, p ≤ 0.05 compared to EV DMSO, #, p ≤ 0.05 compared to EV B[a]P. d NHEKs were treated with 20 ng/ml AREG for 24 h and AKR1C3 transcript level were analyzed by qRT-PCR. e Western blot analyses of SRC and its phosphorylated form (Y416).

evidence.claim: Antibody identifiers for anti-SRC detection: mouse monoclonal anti-SRC clone 60315-1-lg (Proteintech, cat# 60315-1-lg) and rabbit anti-phospho-Y416-SRC clone D49G4 (#6943, Cell Signaling Technology). These antibody identifiers are needed for downstream MethodObservation.antibodies[] population and cross-reactivity assessment for any SRC surface/WB assay performed in this paper.

spans.quote: mouse monoclonal anti-PCNA (NA03, Merck, Darmstadt, Germany); rabbit anti-GLUT1 (ab652; Abcam, Cambridge, UK); goat anti-GLUT1 (sc-1603; Santa Cruz Biotechnology, Santa Cruz, CA, USA); mouse monoclonal anti-RAC1 (05-389, Millipore, Burlington, MA, USA); mouse monoclonal anti-SRC antibody (60315-1-lg, Proteintech, Rosemont, IL, USA); rabbit anti-phospho Y416-SRC antibody D49G4 (#6943, Cell Signaling, Danvers, MA, USA); mouse monoclonal anti-VAV2 antibody (sc-271442, Santa Cruz Biotechnology, Santa Cruz, CA, USA)

evidence.claim: In osteoblast-like MC3T3-E1 cells, fluid shear stress increases c-Src activation (Tyr416 phosphorylation) and promotes redistribution of RANKL toward the cell periphery, with increased RANKL in the membrane fraction assessed by subcellular fractionation. c-Src activation is associated with membrane translocation of cargo, providing indirect evidence that activated c-Src participates in plasma membrane-proximal signaling. This is not a direct surface-accessibility assay for SRC itself, but is relevant as a non-surface expression context (SRC activity at membrane fraction detected by fractionation + WB).

spans.quote: Fluid shear stress increased c-Src activation (Tyr416 phosphorylation) and promoted redistribution of RANKL toward the cell periphery, accompanied by an increase of RANKL in the membrane fraction.

evidence.claim: Definitional statement that the cell surfaceome encompasses proteins tethered to the extracellular leaflet of the plasma membrane as well as secreted and shed proteins that remain tightly cell-associated. This contextualizes the scope of surfaceome studies that detected SRC: proteins enriched by cell-surface capture MS may include shed/secreted proteins co-purified with the membrane fraction, which is relevant to interpreting the ambiguous SRC surfaceome detections.

spans.quote: The cell surfaceome encompasses all cell surface proteins tethered to the extracellular leaflet of the plasma membrane, as well as a portion of secreted and shed proteins that may stay tightly associated with the cell.

evidence.claim: Topology anchor (duplicate suppressed — covered above as topology row). Retained here as a cross-reference note only; the primary selection is the topology row.

spans.quote: These motifs are phosphorylated by SRC-family tyrosine kinases, which are tethered via acyl groups to the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane.

evidence.claim: SRC is noncanonically translocated and inverted onto the outer cell surface in cancer, both in cancer cell lines (in vitro) and in primary tumors (in vivo). This represents a disease-state-induced surface-exposure event in which the normally cytoplasmic kinase acquires an extracellular membrane-associated topology, creating a cancer-specific surface antigen (eSrc). This is a fundamentally novel surface localization distinct from its canonical inner-leaflet / cytoplasmic-face membrane association.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: cancer cell lines and primary tumor specimens

cell_context.material_kind_other_label: cancer cell lines and primary tumors (in vitro and in vivo)

spans.quote: In this work, we found that Src is noncanonically translocated and inverted onto the cell surface in cancer, both in vitro and in vivo.

evidence.claim: Autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE) is identified as the secretory mechanism by which SRC is delivered to the outer cell surface in cancer cells. This disease-state-induced accessibility modulation — from intracellular/inner-leaflet reservoir to extracellular-facing surface pool — is mechanistically driven by ALE and is prominent in cancer cell lines.

spans.quote: We identified autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE) as a secretory mechanism prominent in cancer cell lines.

evidence.claim: Extracellular membrane-associated SRC (eSrc) is detected in primary tumors, confirming that cancer-state-induced surface exposure of SRC occurs in clinical tissue, not just cell lines. Anti-Src antibody-based therapies mediated tumor cell killing in cell culture and mouse xenograft models, validating eSrc as a functionally accessible cancer surface antigen.

spans.quote: Furthermore, this extracellular membrane-associated Src (eSrc) was found in primary tumors, and anti-Src antibody-based therapies mediated tumor cell killing in cell culture systems and in mouse xenograft models.

evidence.claim: Exocytosis exposes SRC at the outer surface of cancer cells, placing it in a position accessible for therapeutic targeting. This review-level assertion corroborates the primary findings from PMID:41818370 regarding cancer-cell-specific surface exposure of SRC via exocytosis.

spans.quote: Exocytosis exposes Src at the outer surface of cancer cells, poised for therapeutic targeting.

evidence.claim: SRC-family kinases, including SRC, are tethered via acyl groups (N-myristoylation) to the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane. This inner-leaflet membrane association is the canonical subcellular localization — cytoplasmic face of the plasma membrane — distinct from transmembrane or extracellular topologies. In resting, non-cancer cells SRC is therefore NOT accessible from the extracellular side.

spans.quote: These motifs are phosphorylated by SRC-family tyrosine kinases, which are tethered via acyl groups to the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane.

evidence.claim: In resting T cells, constitutive tyrosine phosphatase activity (CD45, CD148) suppresses TCR triggering. SRC-family kinases are present in regions of close membrane contact during TCR-pMHC engagement, where large phosphatases are excluded but SRC-kinases are not. This establishes a cell-state context (TCR-activated vs resting T cells) in which SRC-family kinase accessibility at the inner plasma membrane leaflet is modulated by spatial exclusion of inhibitory phosphatases.

spans.quote: This observation, together with evidence that constitutive tyrosine phosphatase activity suppresses TCR triggering in resting cells 7 , 8 , led to the proposal that TCR binding to pMHC induced tyrosine phosphorylation of the TCR by trapping it in small regions of close contact which exclude large receptor tyrosine phosphatases CD45 and CD148 but not the SRC-kinases 9 .

evidence.claim: c-Src is expressed in osteoblast-like MC3T3-E1 cells (murine osteoblastic lineage) and is the subject of mechanical-stress modulation studies in this cell type. This establishes osteoblast lineage as a cell type where SRC is functionally expressed and responsive to biomechanical stimuli.

spans.quote: Here, we tested whether fluid shear stress alters RANKL subcellular distribution in osteoblast-like MC3T3-E1 cells and examined the involvement of the non-receptor tyrosine kinase c-Src.

evidence.claim: In osteoblast-like MC3T3-E1 cells, fluid shear stress (mechanical stimulation) activates c-Src (Tyr416 phosphorylation) and promotes redistribution of RANKL toward the cell periphery with an increase in RANKL in the membrane fraction. This represents a mechanically-induced accessibility modulation state in osteoblasts where SRC activation is the upstream driver of surface translocation of a downstream target (RANKL). Baseline: resting osteoblasts with intracellular RANKL. Modulating state: fluid shear stress. Change: SRC activation → RANKL peripheral/membrane redistribution.

spans.quote: Fluid shear stress increased c-Src activation (Tyr416 phosphorylation) and promoted redistribution of RANKL toward the cell periphery, accompanied by an increase of RANKL in the membrane fraction.

evidence.claim: Integrated model from osteoblast shear-stress study: mechanical stimulation activates c-Src, which facilitates translocation of RANKL from intracellular lysosome-like stores toward membrane-proximal regions in osteoblastic cells. This positions SRC as a key regulator of membrane-accessible pools of signaling proteins in mechanically stimulated bone cells, with SRC kinase activity (not surface localization per se) being the modulating event.

spans.quote: Together, these data support a model in which shear stress activates c-Src to facilitate RANKL localization from intracellular stores toward membrane-proximal regions, thereby providing a mechanistic link between mechanical cues and osteoblast-derived osteoclastogenic signaling.

evidence.claim: SRC kinase is identified within a surfaceome cluster (cluster 2) in chondrocytes, associated with chondrocyte phenotype control. This places SRC in the surfaceome-proximate proteome of chondrogenic cells (cartilage tissue), though SRC itself lacks transmembrane domains and the reference likely reflects inner-leaflet membrane association or indirect surfaceome co-clustering.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: embryonic chick limb bud chondrogenic cells

spans.quote: Surfaceome cluster 2 harbors several cell-surface regulators of chondrocyte biology, including RECK (a membrane-anchored matrix metalloprotease inhibitor [ 45 ]), FGFR2 (fibroblast growth factor receptor 2; regulates chondrocyte proliferation/differentiation signaling [ 46 ]), multiple ephrin receptors (EPHA7, EPHB2, EPHB1, EPHB3 with potential roles in cartilage morphogenesis [ 47 ]), SRC kinase (chondrocyte phenotype control [ 48 ]), and GPC1 (glypical-1

evidence.claim: SRC is a key component of the SRC/RAC1/PAK1/PIP5K/EZRIN pathway that regulates NIS (sodium/iodide symporter) anchoring to the actin cytoskeleton and retention at the plasma membrane. This establishes SRC as functionally active at the inner-leaflet of the plasma membrane in thyroid-relevant cellular contexts, modulating surface accessibility of NIS by controlling cytoskeletal anchoring.

spans.quote: Biochemical and functional analysis allowed us to establish the SRC/RAC1/PAK1/PIP5K/EZRIN pathway as a key regulator of NIS actin-cytoskeleton anchoring and retention at the PM [ 10 ].

evidence.claim: SRC protein (total and pSRC Y419) is expressed in nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC) cell lines: THY1-expressing NPC cells and THY1-knockdown HK11.19 cells. SRC activation state (pY419) varies by THY1 expression status, establishing NPC cells as a disease context with differential SRC activation across cell states.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: NPC / HK11.19 nasopharyngeal carcinoma cell lines

spans.quote: Western blot analysis of pSRC (Y419), total SRC, and THY1 in ( A ) THY1-expressing NPC cells and ( B ) THY1-knockdown HK11.19 cells were shown.

evidence.claim: SRC protein is enriched in small extracellular vesicles (sEVs) secreted by glioblastoma (GBM) cell lines with high invadopodia activity (Cluster 2 proteins). This indicates that in GBM cells with active invadopodia formation, SRC is mobilized to secreted vesicle fractions, representing a disease-state-associated redistribution of SRC away from the inner-leaflet plasma membrane toward extracellular vesicle secretion in invasive glioblastoma.

spans.quote: Furthermore, these high invadopodia activity cell lines secreted sEVs with a greater abundance of proteins involved in the regulation of invadopodia formation (CTTN, CFL1, SRC, ITGA3, ITGB3 – Cluster 2) and proteolytic activity (MMP2, MMP14, BSG/CD147 – Cluster 2) (Fig. 3 D).

evidence.claim: SRC protein (total and pSRC Y416) is expressed and phosphorylation-modulated in human keratinocyte cell lines (HaCaT) and normal human epidermal keratinocytes (NHEKs), with SRC activity modulated by AHR pathway and benzo[a]pyrene treatment. This establishes skin keratinocytes as a cell type with detectable SRC expression and regulated SRC kinase activity.

spans.quote: n = 2, representative pictures. b qRT-PCR analyses of CYP1A1 and AKR1C3 in HaCaT-shAHR and HaCaT-EV cells treated with 10 μM PP2 or 0.1 % DMSO for 24 h. n = 4. *, p ≤ 0.05 compared to EV DMSO, #, p ≤ 0.05 compared to EV B[a]P. c qRT-PCR analyses of CYP1A1 and AKR1C3 in HaCaT-shAHR and HaCaT-EV keratinocytes treated as indicated for 24 h. n = 4–7. *, p ≤ 0.05 compared to EV DMSO, #, p ≤ 0.05 compared to EV B[a]P. d NHEKs were treated with 20 ng/ml AREG for 24 h and AKR1C3 transcript level were analyzed by qRT-PCR. e Western blot analyses of SRC and its phosphorylated form (Y416).

confidence_reasoning: A1 grades this 'conflicting': strong functional evidence for cancer-specific eSrc surface inversion (PMID:41818370, PMID:41818382) from a single recent study cluster conflicts with well-established inner-leaflet canonical topology (PMC11399299). A2 confirms high state dependence — surface accessibility strictly requires oncogenic transformation and active ALE; normal cells are inaccessible. Both A1 contradicting_evidence (severity=high) and A2 accessibility_modulation (category=disease_state_induced) drag confidence below high. The eSrc studies are abstract-level from the same lab/time cluster with limited independent replication, and antibody validation against the inverted kinase epitope is not documented in the ledger, warranting moderate rather than low confidence given preclinical xenograft functional data.
